verses.text: As Jacob travels south from Laban, the angels of God meet him. He names the place Mahanaim — two camps. His mortal company and a heavenly one are walking the same road. He doesn't see his protection often, but it is there.

As Jacob travels south from Laban, the angels of God meet him. He names the place Mahanaim — two camps. His mortal company and a heavenly one are walking the same road. He doesn't see his protection often, but it is there.

Jacob sends messengers ahead to Esau in Edom. The message is rehearsed and humble: thy servant Jacob says — I have lived with Laban, gained livestock and servants, and have sent word in hope of finding grace in thy sight. The supplanter calls himself servant for the first time.

Jacob sends messengers ahead to Esau in Edom. The message is rehearsed and humble: thy servant Jacob says — I have lived with Laban, gained livestock and servants, and have sent word in hope of finding grace in thy sight. The supplanter calls himself servant for the first time.

Jacob sends messengers ahead to Esau in Edom. The message is rehearsed and humble: thy servant Jacob says — I have lived with Laban, gained livestock and servants, and have sent word in hope of finding grace in thy sight. The supplanter calls himself servant for the first time.
The messengers return: he is coming to meet you — with four hundred men. The number lands like a hammer. Esau is not coming alone.

Jacob is greatly afraid and distressed. He divides his people, flocks, and herds into two camps — if Esau attacks one, the other can flee. Fear plans for the worst case, even alongside the angels he just saw.

Jacob is greatly afraid and distressed. He divides his people, flocks, and herds into two camps — if Esau attacks one, the other can flee. Fear plans for the worst case, even alongside the angels he just saw.

Then Jacob prays. O God of my father Abraham, God of my father Isaac, LORD who said return. I am not worthy of the least of your mercies. With only a staff I crossed this Jordan; now I am two bands. Deliver me — from my brother, from Esau, lest he come and strike us all, mother and children. The first earnest prayer of his life.

Then Jacob prays. O God of my father Abraham, God of my father Isaac, LORD who said return. I am not worthy of the least of your mercies. With only a staff I crossed this Jordan; now I am two bands. Deliver me — from my brother, from Esau, lest he come and strike us all, mother and children. The first earnest prayer of his life.

Then Jacob prays. O God of my father Abraham, God of my father Isaac, LORD who said return. I am not worthy of the least of your mercies. With only a staff I crossed this Jordan; now I am two bands. Deliver me — from my brother, from Esau, lest he come and strike us all, mother and children. The first earnest prayer of his life.

Then Jacob prays. O God of my father Abraham, God of my father Isaac, LORD who said return. I am not worthy of the least of your mercies. With only a staff I crossed this Jordan; now I am two bands. Deliver me — from my brother, from Esau, lest he come and strike us all, mother and children. The first earnest prayer of his life.

That night he prepares a massive gift for Esau: 200 she-goats, 20 he-goats, 200 ewes, 20 rams, 30 milch camels and their colts, 40 cows, 10 bulls, 20 she-donkeys, 10 foals. A small fortune offered to soften an old wound.

That night he prepares a massive gift for Esau: 200 she-goats, 20 he-goats, 200 ewes, 20 rams, 30 milch camels and their colts, 40 cows, 10 bulls, 20 she-donkeys, 10 foals. A small fortune offered to soften an old wound.

That night he prepares a massive gift for Esau: 200 she-goats, 20 he-goats, 200 ewes, 20 rams, 30 milch camels and their colts, 40 cows, 10 bulls, 20 she-donkeys, 10 foals. A small fortune offered to soften an old wound.

He sends the animals ahead in droves, with space between each. He coaches each servant: when Esau meets you and asks whose you are and where you go, say — your servant Jacob's, a gift to my lord Esau; he is behind us.

He sends the animals ahead in droves, with space between each. He coaches each servant: when Esau meets you and asks whose you are and where you go, say — your servant Jacob's, a gift to my lord Esau; he is behind us.

He sends the animals ahead in droves, with space between each. He coaches each servant: when Esau meets you and asks whose you are and where you go, say — your servant Jacob's, a gift to my lord Esau; he is behind us.

Each successive drove gets the same instruction. The pattern is repeated for emphasis: behold, your servant Jacob is behind us. Jacob hopes to appease Esau with what arrives before him. Perhaps he will accept me when he sees my face.

Each successive drove gets the same instruction. The pattern is repeated for emphasis: behold, your servant Jacob is behind us. Jacob hopes to appease Esau with what arrives before him. Perhaps he will accept me when he sees my face.

Each successive drove gets the same instruction. The pattern is repeated for emphasis: behold, your servant Jacob is behind us. Jacob hopes to appease Esau with what arrives before him. Perhaps he will accept me when he sees my face.

That night he rises, takes his two wives, his two handmaids, and his eleven sons, and crosses the ford of the Jabbok. He sends all his possessions across, too. By the end he is alone on the wrong bank.

That night he rises, takes his two wives, his two handmaids, and his eleven sons, and crosses the ford of the Jabbok. He sends all his possessions across, too. By the end he is alone on the wrong bank.
Jacob is left alone. And a man wrestles with him until the breaking of the day. No name is given to the man. The fight goes on all night.
When the man sees he cannot prevail, he touches the socket of Jacob's hip — and Jacob's hip is out of joint. The whole match was being held by the one who could end it with a single touch.
Let me go, for the day breaketh. Jacob answers, gripping: I will not let thee go, except thou bless me. The man who once stole blessings now refuses to be unblessed.

What is thy name? Jacob — supplanter. No longer. Your name shall be Israel — meaning he strives with God — for you have wrestled with God and with men, and have prevailed. The new name is given by the very one he wrestled.

What is thy name? Jacob — supplanter. No longer. Your name shall be Israel — meaning he strives with God — for you have wrestled with God and with men, and have prevailed. The new name is given by the very one he wrestled.
Jacob asks the man's name. Wherefore is it that thou dost ask my name? No answer — and yet he blesses Jacob there. Some questions don't need answers; the blessing is enough.
Jacob names the place Peniel — face of God. I have seen God face to face, and my life is preserved. The fight was with God, and somehow he is still alive.
The sun rises as he leaves Penuel — and he limps. The transformation is real. Israel walks out of the night, but he walks slower than Jacob did.
Even today, the children of Israel do not eat the sinew of the hip — a meal-time memorial to the night their father met God and was wounded and renamed.
